problem_summary: 2016 MCM/ICM Problem C (data insight): The Goodgrant Foundation has $100M/year for 5 years to donate to U.S. colleges/universities to maximize educational impact. Teams were given a ~3000-school candidate list and a College Scorecard dataset with ~95 attributes per school, and had to decide which schools to fund, how much, and over what time horizon — building an investment-return measure from messy, missing-data-heavy real data. This O-award team framed it as a financial-portfolio optimization: define a charity-specific ROI, attach a "risk" measure, and solve a constrained allocation via integer programming over 5 years.
models_used: Data screening / filtering pipeline (drop non-operating schools, HCM2-flagged schools, schools with >50% NULL attributes; merge binary/redundant columns like net price and retention rate) reducing 7805 schools / 95 attrs to ~2700 schools / ~60 attrs

K-means clustering for missing-data imputation (cluster on complete 'integral' columns like PREDDEG, CONTROL, HBCU, PCIP subject percentages, UGDS demographics, PCTFLOAN; fill missing values with cluster mean) — K selected via R^2 (between/total sum of squares), optimal K=5
Min-max normalization to [0,1] for cross-attribute comparability

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for attribute selection / redundancy removal — e.g., found md_earn_wne_p6 explains 95% of the salary group so it represents Salary; also used PCA-derived explained-variance shares (31/13/56%) as importance inputs for Urgency weighting

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to assign weights to Output sub-factors via pairwise-comparison matrix; consistency check CI=0.0113<0.10 (and a second vector V2 for schools lacking SAT scores, CI passes)

Custom ROI evaluation system: ROI = (Output / Input) x Urgency, where Output = weighted [Salary After Graduation, Retention Rate, Repayment Ability, Education Enhance Rate], Input = (1-alpha) + alpha*NetPrice with alpha=0.25, Urgency = weighted [Pell Grant %, Federal Loan %, median Debt]
Risk measure borrowed from Modern Portfolio Theory: coefficient of variation r = sigma_e / mu_e of earnings (md_earn_wne_p6)
Grey Prediction model GM(1,1) (accumulated generating operation + first-order ODE, least-squares parameter fit) to forecast each school's ROI for 2016-2020 from 2009-2013 data

Basic Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP): maximize sum(ROI_i * x_i) subject to budget, weighted-risk cap, school-count bounds (10-30), per-school min/max funding linked to binary y_i, and minimum student-body size; solved with IBM ILOG CPLEX 12.6 via Python API -> 14 schools

Time-series MILP extension over t in {1..5} (decision vars x_i^t, y_i^t) with per-year count bounds and a per-year budget upper/lower bound (uneven distribution allowed) -> 16 schools with year-by-year allocation
key_techniques: Reframing a charity-donation problem as a portfolio-investment problem — explicit cross-domain transfer from finance (ROI, Modern Portfolio Theory, risk-return efficient frontier) onto educational data

Decomposing a vague objective ('educational performance') into a three-part composite metric (efficiency via Output/Input, plus 'Urgency' as a charity-specific correction factor) so the abstract goal becomes computable from real columns
Layered weighting: PCA to prune correlated variables, then AHP pairwise comparison to set defensible weights, with consistency checks reported as evidence of rigor

Engineering an 'Education Enhance Rate' feature (normalized earnings minus normalized SAT, re-normalized) as a value-added proxy for how much a school lifts students — a constructed feature, not a raw column
Correction-coefficient guarding: alpha=0.25 damping on Net Price to stop a single low-input variable from dominating and trivially favoring cheap schools
Two-tier model strategy: a clean static MILP first to establish the mechanism, then a time-indexed MILP that also outputs WHEN to fund each school
Solving with an industrial optimizer (CPLEX) rather than a hand-rolled heuristic — credible global-optimal claims

Sensitivity analysis along three independent axes: risk cap vs. max return (found saturation near risk~0.82), number-of-schools vs. return (peak ~12 schools, validating the 10-30 constraint), and 4 distribution policies (all-year-1 / even / bounded-uneven / unconstrained), justifying their chosen policy by showing it is within 3.5% of the unconstrained optimum while staying practical
why_it_won: "It tells one coherent, end-to-end story — raw messy data -> screening -> imputation -> normalization -> a justified composite ROI -> risk -> optimization -> time-phased schedule -> sensitivity — with every stage motivated and connected to the next. The judge-pleasing move is the finance analogy: by mapping charity donation onto Modern Portfolio Theory (return, risk as coefficient of variation, constrained allocation), the team converts an open-ended 'who should we fund' question into a crisp, solvable optimization with a concrete, defensible deliverable (a ranked list AND a 5-year, school-by-school dollar schedule). The data-engineering is unusually disciplined for an MCM paper: it actually grapples with the >50% NULL columns, HCM2 flags, and redundant binary attributes that the real Scorecard data contains, then uses clustering-based imputation with a principled K-selection (R^2), rather than hand-waving the data. Method choices are layered and cross-checked (PCA + AHP + reported consistency indices), giving an aura of rigor. And crucially they answer the actual deliverable: a specific 16-school list plus the year-by-year allocation table, solved to global optimum with CPLEX, then stress-tested with three separate sensitivity studies that each loop back to validate a modeling choice."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet (line 9-21): compresses the entire pipeline (screening -> K-means imputation -> PCA/AHP ROI -> portfolio-theory risk -> MILP basic + time-series with Grey prediction -> sensitivity) into one paragraph and states concrete results (14 schools basic, 16 schools time-series). Section 2 Data Processing (lines 102-221): the strongest section — explicit thresholds (50% NULL cutoff), named columns kept/dropped, K-means imputation with R^2-based K selection and an actual clustering output table (5 classes, sizes, imputed md_earn_wne_p10 values). Section 3 ROI Evaluation System (lines 223-318): defines ROI=Output/Input x Urgency, derives each sub-factor from named real columns, shows the AHP pairwise matrix and weight vector V1=[0.576,0.125,0.077,0.222] with CI=0.0113, and includes a flowchart visualizing the metric. Section 4 (lines 412-571): full MILP formulation (objective + 5 constraint families with explicit constants S_min=1000, A_i^min, b), CPLEX solution, and the 16-school x 5-year allocation table (Figure 5) that directly answers the prompt. Section 5 (lines 573-637): three sensitivity analyses, each closing a loop back to a modeling decision (risk saturation ~0.82; optimal ~12 schools; policy-3 within 3.5% of unconstrained). The closing letter to 'Mr. Alpha Chiang' (lines 689-717) restates ROI and names all 16 schools in plain language."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy subjectivity the team itself flags: AHP weights, the alpha=0.25 Input correction, index choices, and constraint bounds are set 'according to experience and intuition' with no objective grounding or validation data

The recommended schools are not intuitively sensible — the team admits in Future Work 'the chosen university is not very ideal. We cannot figure out intuitively why we should invest them.' The basic-model top picks (e.g., Uta Mesivta of Kiryas Joel, several Johnson & Wales campuses, Talmudical seminaries) suggest the ROI metric is being gamed by low-Net-Price / niche schools rather than reflecting real impact — a face-validity failure

Constraint typos/inconsistencies in the formulation: eq. (25)/(31) write A_i^min y_i <= x_i <= A_i^min y_i (max bound should be A_i^max), and eq. (26)/(32) write s_i y_i <= S_min which is backwards for a MINIMUM-size filter (should be s_i y_i >= S_min). The math as printed is wrong even if the implementation was right
No genuine validation: there is no held-out data, no ground-truth outcome, no back-test of whether high-ROI schools actually produced better results — 'we have no data to validate our model'

Grey GM(1,1) is applied to ROI time series, but the per-school 5-year historical ROI inputs (2009-2013) aren't clearly shown to exist in the provided data; the forecast figures are sparse/illustrative and the claim 'better than Linear Fitting' is asserted, not demonstrated

Static feedback loop ignored: the model treats school ROI as exogenous and unaffected by the donation itself, which contradicts the whole point of charitable investment (they acknowledge this as a weakness — 'Neglect of Future Dynamics')
Imputation assumes one global K-means clustering on integral columns transfers validly to every missing variable — a strong, unverified assumption that could bias filled values

Some figures are low-quality / inconsistent (e.g., grey-prediction plots with mismatched year axes; a pie/bar with a malformed row '13 | 6 | 6'), and the time-series budget row sums to per-year totals that don't obviously reconcile to a clean $100M/yr x 5 = $500M plan
reusable_patterns: Composite-index recipe for data problems: build a named, decomposable score (here ROI = Output/Input x Urgency) where each component maps to specific real columns, prune correlated inputs with PCA, then weight with AHP and REPORT the consistency index as rigor evidence

Cross-domain framing as a winning device: borrow a mature theory from an adjacent field (finance/Modern Portfolio Theory) to give an open-ended optimization a ready-made vocabulary of objective, risk, and constraints
Risk = coefficient of variation (sigma/mu) as a cheap, scale-free risk proxy when only one fluctuating output variable is available
Two-stage model ladder: ship a clean static optimization to establish the mechanism, then add a time index for the realistic multi-period version — lets graders see both clarity and sophistication

Formulate selection-and-allocation jointly as MILP with a binary 'is it chosen' var y_i linked to a continuous 'how much' var x_i via big-M-style coupling constraints, plus count and budget caps; solve with CPLEX/Gurobi for credible global-optimal claims

Disciplined data-cleaning narrative: state explicit numeric thresholds (e.g., drop columns/rows >50% missing), name exactly which columns are merged/kept/dropped and why, and show a before/after count — this reads as serious and is easy to grade
Clustering-based imputation with principled K selection via an R^2 (between-class / total variance) sweep, presented with the actual cluster-size and fill-value table

Sensitivity analysis that closes loops: design each sweep so its result validates a specific earlier choice (risk cap -> saturation point; school count -> confirms your range; distribution policy -> your chosen policy is near-optimal AND practical), rather than reporting sensitivity for its own sake
Add a problem-specific correction factor (here 'Urgency' for charity need, and the alpha damping on a lone dominant variable) to adapt a borrowed metric to the contest's actual objective
Honest, specific weaknesses section (subjectivity, no validation data, non-intuitive picks, ignored dynamics) — naming real flaws concretely reads as credibility, not weakness, to judges
